blocks.content: They went back to the canvas. Cassandra's cloud had been improved by accident and she was now working on the awning, which was going better.

Ethan sat on the edge of the paint table and watched her work. She had the focused expression she got when she was trying to do something precisely — a slight pull between her brows, her breath held for a second before each stroke.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You're watching me.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yes.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Is that a habit too?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Only certain things.
She added a careful line of yellow to the awning's edge.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: When did you know you could do this?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I don't know if I can do it. I know I can't not do it.
She lowered the brush.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: There's a gap between how something looks and what it is. When I try to paint a tree, I draw what I think a tree should look like. But you draw what the tree actually is.
He looked at her.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That's exactly it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I understand that gap.
She said it quietly. Not an explanation. Just the statement, placed between them like something being set down carefully.
He felt the weight of it — the sense that she meant more than painting. He didn't ask. He didn't push. He let it be what it was.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I think you're better than you think you are.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: At painting?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: At most things.
She turned to look at him — a real look, the kind that landed. Outside, the rain had softened to something quieter.
